name: acidulate
description: To use an acid (such as that found in citrus juice, vinegar, or wine) to prevent browning, alter flavour, or make an item safe for canning.

name: al dente
description: To cook food (typically pasta) to the point where it is tender but not mushy.

name: amandine
description: A culinary term indicating a garnish of almonds. A dish served amandine is usually cooked with butter and seasonings, then sprinkled with whole or flaked, toasted almonds.

name: anti-griddle
description: A kitchen appliance that flash freezes or semi-freezes foods placed on its chilled metal top.

name: aspic
description: A savoury gelatin made from meat stock or consomme, and often shaped in a mold. Foods served in aspic are suspended in or on top of the gelatin.

name: au gratin
description: Prepared in the gratin style. Foods served au gratin are topped with breadcrumbs or cheese then browned under a broiler.

name: au jus
description: Foods served au jus, typically meat or sandwiches, are served with an unthickened sauce made from roast meat drippings, commonly in a separate side dish.

name: au poivre
description: Foods served au poivre, typically steak, are crusted with ground black pepper prior to cooking.

name: backwoods cooking
description: A method of cooking without the use of utensils that commonly takes place in remote areas, often in combination with wild or conventional camping.

name: baghaar
description: A cooking technique used in Pakistani cuisine and Indian cuisine in which cooking oil is heated and spices are added to fry. The oil is then added to a dish for flavoring.

name: bain-marie
description: A method of cooking where a container of food is placed in or above boiling water in order to heat gradually or to keep warm.

name: baking
description: A dry-heat cooking method, usually in an oven, that uses convection rather than direct heat.

name: barding
description: Wrapping meat in fat prior to roasting.

name: barbecuing
description: Cooking meat or fish slowly over a barbecue grill with indirect heat and smoke.

name: basting
description: Periodically pouring liquid over food as it roasts.

name: blanching
description: A technique by which a fresh food such as a vegetable or fruit is briefly immersed in boiling water, removed after a timed interval, and then plunged into iced water or rinsed with cold running water (shocking or refreshing) to halt the cooking process.

name: boiling
description: A wet cooking method where the food is cooked in boiling water.

name: braising
description: A combination-cooking method that uses both wet and dry heats: typically, the food is first seared at a high temperature, then finished in a covered pot at a lower temperature while sitting in some (variable) amount of liquid (which may also add flavor).

name: bricolage
description: The preparation of a meal from whatever ingredients happen to be on hand.

name: brine
description: To soak a food item in salted water.

name: broasting
description: A method of cooking chicken and other foods using a pressure fryer and condiments.

name: browning
description: The process of partially cooking the surface of meat to help remove excessive fat and to give the meat a brown color crust and flavor through various browning reactions.

name: candy making
description: The preparation of candies and sugar confections by dissolving sugar in water or milk to form a syrup, which is boiled until it reaches the desired concentration or starts to caramelize.

name: caramelization
description: The browning of sugar, a process used extensively in cooking for the resulting nutty flavor and brown color.

name: carryover cooking
description: The phenomenon by which food retains heat and continues to cook even after being removed from the source of heat.

name: cartouche
description: Cooking technique involving a round piece of parchment paper placed directly on food to retain moisture, prevent skin formation, and promote even cooking

name: casserole
description: Food cooked and served in a casserole dish.

name: charbroiler
description: A cooking device consisting of a series of grates or ribs that can be heated using a variety of means, and is used in both residential and commercial applications for a variety of cooking operations.

name: cheesemaking
description: The craft of making cheese.

name: chiffonade
description: To cut leaves into long thin strips.

name: red cooking
description: A slow braising technique that imparts a red color to the prepared food, frequently used in Chinese cuisine.

name: clay pot cooking
description: A process of cooking food in a pot made from unglazed and natural clay.

name: coddling
description: Heating food in water kept just below the boiling point. Coddled egg may be prepared using this method.

name: concasse
description: To rough chop any ingredient, especially vegetables. The term is particularly applied to tomatoes, where tomato concasse is a tomato that has been peeled, seeded (seeds and skins removed), and chopped to specified dimensions.

name: conche
description: A surface-scraping mixer and agitator that evenly distributes cocoa butter within chocolate, and may act as a "polisher" of the particles.

name: confit
description: A generic term for various kinds of food that have been cooked in grease, oil, or sugar water (syrup).

name: consomme
description: A type of clear soup made from richly flavored stock or bouillon that has been clarified.

name: cooking with alcohol
description: Many dishes incorporate alcoholic beverages into the food itself.

name: cream
description: The butterfat-heavy portion of whole milk that, due to its fat content, separates from the milk and rises to the top.

name: creaming
description: Either combining ingredients (typically butter and sugar) into a smooth paste or cooking meat or vegetables in a thick dairy-based sauce.

name: croquette
description: A small roll made of finely chopped meat and/or vegetables that is breaded and fried.

name: culinary triangle
description: A concept described by anthropologist Claude Levi-Strauss involving three types of cooking: boiling, roasting, and smoking, usually done to meat.

name: curdling
description: The breaking of an emulsion or colloid into large parts of different composition through the physico-chemical processes of flocculation, creaming, and coalescence. Curdling is intentional and desirable in making cheese and tofu, but may be unintentional and undesirable in making other foods such as sauces and custards.

name: cured fish
description: Fish preserved by fermentation, pickling, smoking, or some combination of these techniques.

name: curing
description: Any of a wide variety of food preservation and flavoring processes used for foods such as meat, fish, and vegetables, by the addition of a combination of salt, nitrates, nitrite, or sugar. Many curing processes also involve smoking, the process of flavoring, or cooking. The use of food dehydration was the earliest form of food curing.

name: deep frying
description: A technique by which a food is completely submerged in hot fat or oil (as opposed to ordinary frying, which involves placing the food in a shallow pool of oil).

name: deglazing
description: To remove fond from the bottom of a pan by adding liquid, usually in the form of stock, wine, or water.

name: degreasing
description: Degreasing is the process of removing excess fat from stocks, soups, sauces, or braising liquids to improve texture and flavor. Common techniques include skimming with a spoon, using a fat-separator cup, or chilling the liquid to solidify fat for easy removal.

name: dough sheeting
description: A technique used in industrial bakeries that involves rolling out dough into a (consistent) dough sheet with a desired even thickness prior to baking.

name: dredging
description: Coating the exterior of a food with a dry material (such as breadcrumbs) prior to cooking.

name: dry roasting
description: Dry roasting is a technique that applies heat to food, primarily nuts, seeds, and spices, without any oil, fat, or water, often in a hot pan, wok, or oven.

name: drying
description: Any of a variety of processes by which a food is preserved by removing moisture, often by the use of a modern food dehydrator or by the traditional method of allowing sunlight and fresh air to evaporate moisture.

name: dum pukht
description: A cooking technique associated with the Awadh region of India, in which meat and vegetables are cooked over a very low flame, generally in sealed containers.

name: Dutch oven cooking
description: A Dutch oven is well suited for long, slow cooking, such as in making roasts, stews, and casseroles. Virtually any recipe that can be cooked in a conventional oven can be cooked in a Dutch oven. They are often used in outdoor cooking, such as when camping.

name: earth oven
description: A shallow pit in the ground used to trap heat and bake, smoke, or steam food.

name: egg wash
description: A preparation of beaten eggs, sometimes mixed with another liquid such as water or milk, which is brushed onto the surface of a pastry before baking.

name: emulsify
description: To combine two liquids that have a natural tendency to separate (such as oil and vinegar) into one homogeneous mass.

name: en papillote
description: A technique by which a food is put into a folded pouch or parcel and then baked.

name: en vessie
description: A cooking method by which a meat or other dish is cooked inside an animal bladder, often a pig bladder.

name: engastration
description: A cooking method by which the cook stuffs the remains of one animal into another animal.

name: engine cooking
description: Cooking food from the excess heat of an internal combustion engine, typically the engine of a car or a truck.

name: escagraph
description: Writing made out of food.

name: fermentation
description: Fermentation is a culinary technique using microorganisms (bacteria, yeast, or mold) to break down carbohydrates in food, producing acids or alcohol that preserve, enhance flavor, and improve digestibility.

name: fillet
description: To remove bones from meat or fish.

name: flambe
description: To pour alcohol over food and then ignite.

name: flattop grill
description: Refers to cooking that is taken place over a flat-top grill.

name: foam
description: A gelling or stabilizing agent in which air is suspended, creating a light, "fluffy" edible substance, e.g. whipped cream, meringue, and mousse.

name: canning
description: Involves the cooking of foods in sealed cans, among other processes.

name: fondue
description: A communal cooking technique involving dipping foods into a central pot of melted cheese, hot oil/broth, or chocolate.

name: fricassee
description: A stew made with pieces of meat that have been browned in butter, then served in a sauce flavored with the cooking stock.

name: frosting
description: The act of applying icing to the exterior of a baked good (such as a cake or cookie).

name: frying
description: To cook food in oil.

name: chicken frying
description: Battering and pan-frying a piece of beefsteak

name: garnish
description: To add a (typically edible) decorative element to a plate of food prior to serving.

name: gentle frying
description: A cooking technique using a small amount of fat to cook delicate foods, such as eggs, fish, or soft vegetables, slowly at lower temperatures. Also called low-temperature shallow frying.

name: glazing
description: Coating the outer surface of a dish by dipping, dripping, or using a brush with a sweet or savory liquid or sauce.

name: gratin
description: A culinary technique in which a dish is topped with a browned crust, often using breadcrumbs, cheese, or egg.

name: grilling
description: A form of cooking that involves heat applied to the surface of food, commonly from above, below or from the side. Usually involves a significant amount of direct, radiant heat.

name: Hangi
description: A traditional New Zealand Maori method of cooking food using heated rocks buried in a pit oven still used for special occasions.

name: hibachi
description: Japanese cooking technique that uses a high-heat flat-top griddle to quickly sear meats, seafood, and vegetables using oil, butter, soy sauce, and sesame oil. Often called teppanyaki in Western restaurants.

name: high-altitude cooking
description: The process of cooking a food or beverage at altitudes well above sea level, where lower atmospheric pressure causes most foods to cook more slowly and may necessitate the use of special cooking techniques.

name: hot salt frying
description: Oil-free cooking technique that cooks food in a bath of hot salt instead of oil

name: indirect grilling
description: A barbecue cooking technique in which the food is placed to the side of or above the heat source instead of directly over the flame.

name: infusion
description: The process of extracting chemical compounds or flavors from plant material in a solvent such as water, oil, or alcohol, by allowing the material to remain suspended in the solvent over time (a process often called steeping). A common example of an infusion is tea, and many herbal teas are prepared in the same way.

name: jugging
description: The process of stewing whole animals, mainly game or fish, for an extended period in a tightly covered container such as a casserole dish or an earthenware jug.

name: juicing
description: The process of extracting juice from plant tissues such as fruit or vegetables.

name: Julienne
description: A culinary knife cut which involves cutting food (typically vegetables) into long thin strips.

name: kalua
description: A traditional Hawaiian cooking method that utilizes an imu, a type of underground oven.

name: karaage
description: A Japanese cooking technique in which various foods - most often chicken, but also other meat and fish - are deep fried in oil, similar to the preparation of tempura, but with a dredge in starch, rather than a liquid batter.

name: kho
description: A cooking technique in Vietnamese cuisine in which a protein source such as fish, shrimp, poultry, pork, beef, or fried tofu is braised on low heat in a mixture of fish sauce, sugar, and water or a water substitute such as young coconut juice. It is similar to stew.

name: kinpira
description: A Japanese cooking style that can be summarized as a technique of "saute and simmer". It is commonly used to cook root vegetables and other foods.

name: larding
description: The act of threading strips of chilled pork fat through a roast.

name: low-temperature cooking
description: A cooking technique that uses temperatures in the range of about 60 to 90 C (140 to 194 F) for a prolonged time to cook food.

name: maceration
description: The process of preparing foods by softening, breaking down into pieces, or extracting its flavors, typically using a liquid.

name: marination
description: The technique of soaking a food in a seasoned, often acidic, liquid (known as a marinade) prior to cooking. Marination is generally used as a means of adding or enhancing flavor or tenderizing tough cuts of meat, and the process can vary greatly in duration. It is similar to but distinct from brining and pickling.

name: microwave cooking
description: The cooking of food in a microwave oven.

name: mincing
description: Technique in which ingredients are cut into small, uniform pieces

name: mother sauces
description: In French cuisine, the five "fundamental" sauces: bechamel, espagnole, veloute, hollandaise, and tomate, as defined by Auguste Escoffier.

name: nappage
description: a glazing technique used in pastry making. The glaze is used to cover fruit on a fruit tart or other baked goods, to make the fruit pieces shiny, prevent them from drying out, and to reduce oxidation (browning of cut fruit).

name: nixtamalization
description: A process for the preparation of maize (corn) or other grain in which the grain is soaked and cooked in an alkaline solution, usually limewater, and then hulled.

name: once-a-month cooking
description: Preparing and cooking all the meals you need for an entire month in a single day.

name: outdoor cooking
description: Cooking in outdoor environments, which often demand specialized techniques and equipment for preparing food. Equipment used includes mess kits and portable stoves, among others.

name: pan frying
description: Characterized by the use of minimal cooking oil or fat (as opposed to shallow frying or deep frying), typically using just enough oil to lubricate the pan.

name: parbaking
description: a cooking technique in which a bread or dough product is partially baked and then rapidly frozen for storage or assembled into a final product.

name: parboiling
description: Partially or incompletely boiling a food, especially as the first step in a longer cooking process. Parboiling involves cooking a food in boiling water only until it begins to soften, removing the food before it is fully cooked. The cooking is then often finished by a different method, such as braising or grilling.

name: pascalization
description: method of preserving and sterilizing food, in which a product is processed under very high pressure, leading to the inactivation of certain microorganisms and enzymes in the food.

name: paste
description: a semi-liquid colloidal suspension, emulsion, or aggregation used in food preparation or eaten directly as a spread

name: pasteurization
description: a process of food preservation in which packaged foods (e.g., milk and fruit juices) are treated with mild heat, usually to less than 100 C (212 F), to eliminate pathogens and extend shelf life.

name: flash pasteurization
description: method of heat pasteurization of perishable beverages like fruit and vegetable juices, beer, wine, and some dairy products such as milk. Compared with other pasteurization processes, it maintains color and flavor better, but some cheeses were found to have varying responses to the process

name: pellicle
description: A skin or coating of proteins on the surface of meat, fish, or poultry, which allows smoke to better adhere to the surface of the meat during the smoking process.

name: pickling
description: process of preserving or extending the shelf life of food by either anaerobic fermentation in brine or immersion in vinegar.

name: poaching
description: technique that involves heating food submerged in a liquid, such as water, milk, stock or wine. Poaching is differentiated from the other "moist heat" cooking methods, such as simmering and boiling, in that it uses a relatively lower temperature (about 70–80 C or 158–176 F).

name: pre-ferment
description: a fermentation starter used in indirect methods of bread making. It may also be called mother dough.

name: pressure cooking
description: The process of cooking food, using water or other cooking liquid, in a sealed vessel known as a pressure cooker, which does not permit air or liquids to escape below a pre-set pressure.

name: pressure frying
description: a variation on pressure cooking where meat and cooking oil are brought to high temperatures while pressure is held high enough to cook the food more quickly.

name: proofing
description: step in the preparation of yeast bread and other baked goods in which the dough is allowed to rest and rise a final time before baking.

name: puree
description: cooked food, usually vegetables, fruits or legumes, that has been ground, pressed, blended or sieved to the consistency of a creamy paste or liquid

name: reconstitution
description: The process of assembling a palatable food product from processed sources (for example, adding water to concentrated juice or forming meat slurry into chicken nuggets).

name: reduction
description: process of thickening and intensifying the flavor of a liquid mixture, such as a soup, sauce, wine or juice, by simmering or boiling.

name: rendering
description: the process of melting down solid animal fat (suet or trimmings) over low heat to separate pure fat from connective tissue and water

name: ricing
description: cooking term meaning to pass food through a food mill or "ricer", which comes in several forms. Creates a very smooth and uniform puree

name: roasting
description: a cooking method that uses dry heat where hot air covers the food, cooking it evenly on all sides with temperatures of at least 150 C (300 F) from an open flame, oven, or other heat source

name: rotisserie
description: also known as spit-roasting, is a style of roasting where meat is skewered on a spit – a long, solid rod used to hold food while it is being cooked over a fire in a fireplace or over a campfire, or roasted in an oven

name: roux
description: A paste-like thickening agent made of equal quantities of flour and fat cooked together to a sandy texture.

name: sauteing
description: method of cooking that uses a relatively small amount of oil or fat in a shallow pan over relatively high heat.

name: score
description: To cut shallow grooves, often in a diamond pattern, into a cut of meat.

name: searing
description: A technique used in grilling, baking, braising, roasting, sauteing, etc., in which the surface of the food (usually meat, poultry, or fish) is cooked at high temperature until a crust forms from browning.

name: shuck
description: To remove the outer casing of a food item, such as an ear of corn or the shell of an oyster.

name: simmering
description: Cooking technique in which foods are cooked in a hot liquid just below the boiling point

name: skimming
description: To remove solids or separated liquids, such as fat from a stock, from the top of a pot with a spoon

name: slow cooker
description: To simmer slowly over a long period of time using a slow cooker

name: smoking
description: process of flavoring, browning, cooking, or preserving food, particularly meat, fish and tea, by exposing it to smoke from burning or smoldering material, most often wood.

name: sous-vide
description: To cook in a hot water bath at a controlled temperature for a long period of time. The intent is to cook the item evenly, ensuring that the inside is properly cooked without overcooking the outside, and to retain moisture.

name: spatchcock
description: Poultry or game that has been prepared for roasting or grilling by removing the backbone (and sometimes the sternum) and flattening it out before cooking.

name: spherification
description: process that employs sodium alginate and either calcium chloride or calcium glucate lactate to shape a liquid into squishy spheres which visually and texturally resemble roe.

name: steaming
description: Method of cooking using steam

name: steeping
description: soaking of an organic solid, such as leaves, in a liquid (usually water) to extract flavours or to soften it.

name: stewing
description: a slow, moist-heat cooking method that tenderizes tough meat cuts by simmering them completely submerged in liquid within a covered pot.

name: stir frying
description: cooking technique in which ingredients are fried in a small amount of very hot oil while being stirred or tossed in a wok

name: stuffing
description: An edible food mixture, often a starch, used to fill a cavity in another food item.

name: sugar panning
description: method for adding a coating to a nucleus, such as confectionery or nuts. It generally involves adding a small object to a large pan which contains a liquid or powder. As the pan spins, a coating forms, which is subsequently dried or set.

name: sweating
description: The gentle heating of vegetables in a little oil or butter, which usually results in tender, sometimes translucent, pieces.

name: Swissing
description: a culinary technique used to tenderize tough cuts of beef (typically round or rump steak) by mechanically pounding or rolling them, often while dredging in flour, to break down connective tissues.

name: tandoor
description: A cylindrical clay or metal oven used in cooking and baking in Southern, Central, and Western Asia,

name: Tataki
description: meat or fish is seared very briefly over a hot flame or in a pan, and can be briefly marinated in vinegar, sliced thin, and seasoned with ginger

name: tempering
description: Either a method of increasing the shine and durability of chocolate couverture, bringing meat to room temperature before cooking; or bringing food up to temperature slowly as in sous vide

name: tenderizing
description: A process to break down collagens in meat to make it more palatable for consumption.

name: thickening
description: Process of adding a thickening agent, such as corn flour, flour, gelatin, or agar-agar to increase the viscosity of a liquid, especially sauces

name: truss
description: To tie the legs and wings of poultry in a way that promotes even cooking

name: velveting
description: A technique which involves coating pieces of raw meat or poultry in a mixture of cornstarch and liquid prior to cooking, frequently used in Chinese cuisine.

name: whip
description: the culinary technique of rapidly beating ingredients—such as cream or egg whites—to incorporate air, increasing volume and creating a light, fluffy, or stiff texture

name: wok cooking
description: The wok is used in a significant amount of cooking methods.

name: zest
description: The colourful outer layer of citrus fruits, often scraped off and used as a flavouring ingredient.
